posts.caption:
With "Couture," @angelinajolie is taking on one of her most personal role yet. “I lost my mother [to breast cancer] and never met my maternal grandmother, as she died young, so I chose to have a double mastectomy about a decade ago,” she tells Bazaar ahead of the movie’s release, which tells the story of a filmmaker who receives a cancer diagnosis while filming a runway show for Paris Fashion Week. “After reading the script, I knew I wanted to be involved. Nobody could have written the film the way Alice did, had they not experienced cancer. She included very subtle but very real things.” In the latest installment of Bazaar's #FirstNowNext questionnaire, the actress shares how her son Maddox helped her prepare for the character, the first movie that made her take herself seriously, and more. Find exclusive details at the link in bio.
-
Photographed by @solvesundsbostudio, Bazaar Dec 2019/January 2020.

posts.caption:
As the UK basks in a heatwave, we bring you a new edition of HELLO! magazine, HOT off the press. There is a full report on all the royal and celebrity appearances at the Ascot races where the Princess of Wales made a triumphant return after three years away. Keeping with the equestrian theme, we have an exclusive shoot and interview with former reality television star and dressage rider Gemma Owen, daughter of former footballer Michael, who talks all about her new challenges and what her dad thinks of her chosen sport. There is also a chat with DJ Fat Tony as he releases his memoirs and reveals his thoughts on the Beckham family feud, as well as a guide to this year’s World Cup WAGs 💫
Can’t wait? You don’t have to. Get every issue the second it drops - before it reaches the shops. Tap the link in bio to join our VIP membership for instant access and exclusive extras. Be ahead of everyone else 🔗

Keeping with the equestrian theme, we have an exclusive shoot and interview with former reality television star and dressage rider Gemma Owen, daughter of former footballer Michael, who talks all about her new challenges and what her dad thinks of her chosen sport. There is also a chat with DJ Fat Tony as he releases his memoirs and reveals his thoughts on the Beckham family feud, as well as a guide to this year’s World Cup WAGs 💫

Can’t wait? You don’t have to. Get every issue the second it drops - before it reaches the shops. Tap the link in bio to join our VIP membership for instant access and exclusive extras. Be ahead of everyone else 🔗

posts.caption:
Last weekend, @lululemon and @zalando celebrated the launch of the Space to Feel collection and campaign with a day designed for movement, mindfulness and connection. ⁠
⁠
Beginning with a boat ride along the Spree to the hidden retreat of Reetshaus, guests eased into intention-setting, yoga, Pilates, immersive sound experiences, and nourishing local food. ⁠
⁠
From then on, classes were hosted by brand ambassadors @bryony.kenndoff, @thepilatesbabe_ and @madymorrison, and experiences like tea-infused refreshments, personalised engravings and moments of pause in nature were on offer. Space to Feel offered exactly what its name promised: a chance to slow down, tune in and reconnect.⁠
⁠
Find out more about what went down by hitting the link in bio.

⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
For any creative, logging off is just as important as locking in — just ask @tolutemi.⁠
⁠
The model and creative director takes us through his perfect Summer Friday in Brooklyn as part of The Clock Out Ritual with @greygoose, a glimpse into how tastemakers across New York unwind after a long week. ⁠
⁠
Inspired by the new Berry Rouge Flavored Vodka, Temi Ibisanmi shows us how he’s indulging the moment after logging off – from getting ready, stops at his neighborhood favorites, capping it off with a Rouge Royale, a Berry Rouge lemonade spritz, with friends. ⁠
⁠
Read more via the link in bio. #greygoosepartner

posts.caption:
Summer doesn’t arrive all at once. It unfolds in moods, moments, and memories. ☀️🌿
@ahsaassy_ steps into the season’s softest chapter, where easy silhouettes and unhurried afternoons become a reminder that slowing down is its own kind of luxury.
In collaboration with @aurelia_womenswear , this explores the evolving moods of summer, from nature’s quiet embrace to the intensity of the afternoon sun, and the effortless cool of golden evenings.✨
This is a celebration of the season in all its shades, beginning with the calm before the heat, where style isn’t about standing out, but feeling completely at home in your own skin.

posts.alt_text:
Memphis Depay has never been defined by football alone.

In the city he now calls home, the Netherlands’ (@onsoranje) all-time leading scorer reflects on life in Brazil, discusses the identity he’s built beyond the pitch, shares why music has become a vital form of self-expression, and introduces his four-legged co-star Drako, who makes a cameo of his own.

Head to the link in bio to read our June digital cover story with @memphisdepay, where we talked fashion, creativity, and staying true to himself just days before his World Cup journey started.

posts.caption:
Introducing our June digital cover star, @memphisdepay.
To some, he’s one of football’s greatest forwards. To others, he’s a musician, designer, all-around entrepreneur and icon who has spent his entire career challenging the idea that athletes should stay in a single lane.
Ahead of what could be his final World Cup, Memphis reflects on identity, creativity, criticism and why football has always been only one part of a much larger story. “My name is bigger than football in what I live, what I do, and what I represent,” he tells us.
Head to the link in our bio to read the full cover story.

posts.caption:
This isn’t the kind of stuffy suiting you’ve come to expect. Former LVMH Prize winner @soshiotsuki has built his label around the idea that tailoring doesn’t have to be rigid. Instead, Soshi Otsuki approaches it with surgical precision, softening the rules through subtle gestures that feel playful without ever losing their sophistication.
After making his runway debut at Pitti Uomo in January, the Japanese designer arrived at Paris Fashion Week today for his first-ever show in the French capital, unveiling his Spring/Summer 2027 collection.
Relaxed suits came paired with wide, cropped ties, ties tucked neatly into shirts, and shirting washed in soft blues, yellows and sandy neutrals. And while the tailoring remained the headline, an unreleased @asics_sportstyle sneaker quietly hinted at what’s next.

For @kikokostadinov SS27, ditching prints and visible hardware, the "EXTROFLEXION" collection focuses entirely on the tension between fabric and form using internal boning and sculpted textiles.⁠
⁠
The runway also debuted major footwear and accessory updates: a lighter, lace-up @crocs collaboration, streamlined @oakley eyewear, and new silhouettes expanding the Kiko lineup. 👟🔥⁠
Video: Joyce Li/Hypebeast
posts.alt_text:
To just play football? That’s never been enough for Memphis @memphisdepay.

From music and fashion to philanthropy and entrepreneurship, the Dutch icon has spent his career challenging expectations — often long before the rest of the sports world caught up.
“My name is bigger than football in what I live, what I do, and what I represent,” he tells Hypebeast.

At 32, and potentially heading into his final World Cup, Memphis reflects on criticism, creativity, and staying true to himself despite the noise.

Read the full digital cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
@jennifer.garner *always* knows what to do (and it usually involves sorghum).
Between starring in summer’s buzziest new series, taking her near-billion dollar company public, and turning ‘Pretend Cooking Show’ into a reality, she still manages to be the nicest woman in Hollywood.
Playing a widowed food blogger in @Peacock’s adaptation of @elinhilderbrand’s bestseller ‘The Five-Star Weekend,’ the beloved actress and entrepreneur channels her inner Ina Garten for a not-so-Instagrammable girls’ weekend on Nantucket with co-stars-turned-IRL besties, @chloesevigny, @morereginahall, @gemmachan and @darcycarden, exploring what it means to grow and develop in middle age.
At 54, the Marvel Hero and supermom reflects on her genre-spanning career, being “total besties” with John Cena, her surprisingly vast knowledge of weaponry, plus the responsibility of celebrities to stand up for working locally for InStyle’s Summer Imagemakers Issue at the link in @instylemagazine’s bio now.

posts.caption:
Yes, it’s L’OFFICIEL! Cosa hanno in comune “Dune: Part Two”, “Furiosa: A Mad Max Saga” e la nuova serie Apple TV “Lucky”? Sono tutti ambientati nel deserto—e tutti vedono protagonista @AnyaTaylorJoy. «Mi diverte sempre, perché non capisco cosa ci sia nei miei capelli, nel mio incarnato o nella larghezza dei miei occhi che mi renda adatta a interpretare una creatura del deserto. Eppure la gente adora mettermi lì», confessa Taylor-Joy a L’OFFICIEL. «Avverto una vera affinità con questi mondi».
Dal pianeta brullo di Arrakis in “Dune” al Wasteland che domina “Furiosa: A Mad Max Saga”, la nostra protagonista di giugno continua a ritrovarsi negli scenari più ostili del cinema contemporaneo. «Il deserto possiede una sacralità propria», osserva. Alla domanda su cosa ami di questi spazi desolati, la risposta è immediata: «L’alba e il tramonto, soprattutto. Perché vedi i colori più straordinari e senti di essere l’unica persona sulla terra».

posts.caption:
Introducing our global cover star, @anyataylorjoy.
“I’ve always been nomadic by nature,” the British-Argentine actor tells L’OFFICIEL. “Now I’ve just fully embraced it as part of who I am. If I have a couple of days off, I’m always like, ‘Ooh, what’s within driving distance?’ If it’s six hours or less, I’m like, ‘Let’s go. Why not?’”
Fresh off of a recent desert road trip, Taylor-Joy—who has cemented her status as a fashion darling through ambassadorships with @Dior and @TiffanyandCo—is preparing for the release of Apple TV’s ‘Lucky,’ a thriller about a con artist caught in the fallout of a multimillion-dollar heist gone wrong. The role marks another chapter in a career spanning ‘The Witch,’ ‘The Queen’s Gambit,’ ‘Furiosa,’ and ‘Dune.’
In ‘Lucky,’ her character is pursued by the FBI and a ruthless mob boss while searching for something more elusive than freedom: stability. “I was so attracted to playing somebody who was just so desperate to stay still and put down roots,” says Taylor-Joy.
Filmed across the desert and inside Las Vegas’s Caesars Palace, the production left its mark. “I have a real soft spot for Caesars Palace now—we’ve been through a lot together,” she laughs.
At the link in bio, read our full conversation with Taylor-Joy on inhabiting new characters, life on the road, and ‘Lucky.’

posts.caption:
Sessant’anni, e di nuovo padrone del proprio nome. Oggi @GiambattistaValli festeggia il compleanno — nato a Roma il 27 giugno 1966 — e lo fa nell’anno in cui ha riacquisito la piena proprietà della sua maison, dopo che Groupe Artémis (la holding della famiglia Pinault) ne aveva preso il controllo di maggioranza nel 2021. Romano di nascita, parigino d’adozione, formatosi tra Capucci, Fendi e Ungaro, Valli ha costruito un linguaggio couture inconfondibile: tulle vertiginosi, volumi che sembrano sospesi, una femminilità che fonde la grazia mediterranea e l’eleganza francese. Uno dei pochi non francesi ammessi alla Chambre Syndicale de la Haute Couture. Tornare a possedere il proprio nome, a sessant’anni, è la più couture delle dichiarazioni. Buon compleanno.
Photo: Getty Images
#GiambattistaValli #LOfficiel #LOfficielItalia

posts.caption:
#KidSuper lascia Parigi (per una volta) e sceglie il calcio. Ieri sera al Nu Stadium di Miami — casa dell’Inter Miami — @KidSuper ha presentato la collezione Primavera/Estate 2027, prima volta che Colm Dillane porta la linea principale fuori da Parigi. Una mossa personale prima che strategica: incastonata tra due partite del Mondiale, la sfilata è un omaggio alla cultura del pallone — i campetti di quartiere, i tifosi, gli incontri casuali, le amicizie che nascono attorno al gioco. Arte, racconto e sport in un’unica visione. Parigi resta casa, ma stavolta si gioca in trasferta.
Photo: Getty Images
#WorldCup #PFW #LOfficiel #LOfficielItalia

posts.caption:
Al photocall londinese di “Spider-Man: Brand New Day”, @Zendaya continua il suo racconto fatto d’archivio e di citazioni, indossando un abito #JohnGalliano Primavera/Estate 1997 dalla collezione ready-to-wear. Stampa cosmica blu notte, schiena nuda attraversata da catene di cristalli, balze asimmetriche che si muovono come onde. Un pezzo da passerella riportato in vita venticinque anni dopo, styling firmato Law Roach, che da settimane trasforma ogni tappa del press tour in un esercizio di cultura della moda. Dal rosso-blu Versace alle galassie di Galliano: ogni look è un indizio, ogni apparizione un capitolo. Nessuno fa red carpet come loro due. Il film esce il 31 luglio.
Photo: Getty Images
#Zendaya #JohnGalliano #LOfficiel #LOfficielItalia

posts.caption:
Anuv Jain’s (@anuvjain) music is what feelings sound like. Love, passion, calm, longing, and so much more… Words may fail, but you’ll always find an Anuv Jain song, or a lyric that resonates with you. This is the power of music, and what Anuv’s rise has always been about.
Be it Husn, Baarishein, Alag Aasmaan, or his latest, Arz Kiya, Anuv’s music has had a chokehold on the youth and has given words to emotions felt by crowds.
He claims to be a yearner, someone who likes to live in the past at times, feels too deeply, and expresses himself with intention. This Music Day, we raise a toast to his music, his success, and his love for the craft.

posts.caption:
Heritage Reimagined: Rethinking Tradition in Architecture, Design, and Culture - Episode 1 of Inside View Season 2, by Architecture+Design and Lifestyle Asia India, powered by Schueco India (@schueco__india)
What does it truly mean to preserve heritage in a world that’s constantly evolving? In the first episode of Inside View Season 2, presented by Schueco, we travel to Chennai to explore how architecture, fashion and design reinterpret tradition for the future. Join architects Arun Prasad (@arunandassociates) and Arvind Rangan (@arvindvarunaassociates), alongside fashion designer Vivek Karunakaran (@vivekarunakaran), as they unpack the memories, materials and ideas that continue to shape contemporary India.
Arun reflects on the memories embedded within built spaces, Arvind shares his perspective on balancing conservation with contemporary practice, while Vivek reveals how India’s rich textile heritage continues to inspire modern expression.
Coming soon!
#InsideView #InsideViewS2 #architectureplusdesign #LifestyleAsiaIndia #schuecoindia

posts.caption:
Introducing our global cover star, @anyataylorjoy.
“I’ve always been nomadic by nature,” the British-Argentine actor tells L’OFFICIEL. “Now I’ve just fully embraced it as part of who I am. If I have a couple of days off, I’m always like, ‘Ooh, what’s within driving distance?’ If it’s six hours or less, I’m like, ‘Let’s go. Why not?’”
Fresh off of a recent desert road trip, Taylor-Joy—who has cemented her status as a fashion darling through ambassadorships with @Dior and @TiffanyandCo—is preparing for the release of Apple TV’s ‘Lucky,’ a thriller about a con artist caught in the fallout of a multimillion-dollar heist gone wrong. The role marks another chapter in a career spanning ‘The Witch,’ ‘The Queen’s Gambit,’ ‘Furiosa,’ and ‘Dune.’
In ‘Lucky,’ her character is pursued by the FBI and a ruthless mob boss while searching for something more elusive than freedom: stability. “I was so attracted to playing somebody who was just so desperate to stay still and put down roots,” says Taylor-Joy.
Filmed across the desert and inside Las Vegas’s Caesars Palace, the production left its mark. “I have a real soft spot for Caesars Palace now—we’ve been through a lot together,” she laughs.
At the link in bio, read our full conversation with Taylor-Joy on inhabiting new characters, life on the road, and ‘Lucky.’

posts.caption:
Welcome to the June issue, where L’OFFICIEL explores travel not as movement, but as a state of mind. Our “nomadic by nature” cover star @AnyaTaylorJoy discusses her love of road trips, filming the new Apple TV series ‘Lucky’ in the Las Vegas desert, and her unique approach to learning her characters. The enigmatic photographer @SzilveszterMako, renowned for his surreal images, cinematic backdrops, and dreamlike scenes, turned his lens on the actress, capturing her shifting world for our shoot.
Elsewhere, the issue focuses on slow travel and the art of pausing. We take you to Grand Cayman, Ischia, and Costa Rica, where hydrotherapy and relaxation define the experience, and across the Canadian Rockies, Arabian Desert, and Australian Outback by rail—the defining way to travel in 2026. Elsewhere, we follow one writer into the next frontier of luxury wellness: sleep optimization.
In Paris, we sit down with Hong Kong–born couturier @RobertWun, who has made a name for himself with dramatic, over-the-top silhouettes; he reminds us that slowing down is sometimes the most radical imaginative act. And in Tbilisi, it’s the creative class who are putting the Georgian capital on the map—and we have your guide to everything that’s happening now.
On the fashion front, Spring/Summer 2026’s finest looks head to Cape Town for a sun-drenched escape, while long-heralded summer heroes—unexpected denim and easy dresses—capture the spirit of going nowhere in good company, this time in Los Angeles’s canyons. Stay tuned for our issue to hit newsstands this month.

posts.caption:
Though the wild tales of nights out at Studio 54 seem increasingly far-fetched nowadays, New York City has always been the epicenter of queer nightlife. Attracting performers, creatives, and like-minded individuals, LGBTQ+ nightlife spaces have historically allowed the community a space to freely express themselves and their interests without mainstream judgment. With the endless nights and outrageous wardrobes also came some of New York and mainstream culture’s biggest names: #AmandaLepore, #RuPaul, #AndyWarhol, and countless others.
While the general society may not know the importance of LGBTQ+ nightlife spaces, these sprawling halls and tiny bars played a pivotal role—especially in the 1960s and ‘70s—in shaping the activism and values that guided the Gay Liberation Movement. These haunts will forever remain an integral part of the LGBTQ+ community.
As New York gets ready to celebrate its annual Pride Parade tomorrow, visit the link in our bio to explore the rich history of queer nightlife in the city.
Photos Getty Images
#newyorkpride #newyorkprideparade

posts.caption:
@WillyChavarria’s Spring/Summer 2027 collection, ‘Comunión,’ is built on the power of togetherness.
Deeply informed by Chicano spirit yet intentionally universal, ‘Comunión’ reflects the brand’s ongoing dialogue around heritage, memory, and collective identity. It speaks to what is inherited across generations—both tangible objects and intangible feeling—carried through family, home, and shared history.
The collection translates these ideas into clothing through softened tailoring, relaxed suiting, and sculptural silhouettes that balance ease with presence. Domestic references emerge through faded tones and familiar textures, reimagined into a palette of confident, expressive color.
“Each garment, each bag, every shoe was designed with warmth. There is a sense of joy in seeing the light. We realize that history has shown us what power we hold when we come together to do good. This is the joy and inspiration that each look holds. We are confident, kind and tough,” @newyorkwilly says.
Photos courtesy of @paulyem and Getty Images
#willychavarria #parisfashionweek #willychavarrianewyork

posts.caption:
July 12, 1998, is remembered as the day France won its first #FIFAWorldCup. It was also the day #YvesSaintLaurent turned the Stade de France into a runway.
Before France faced Brazil in one of football’s most historic matches, the designer transformed the stadium into the setting for a 15-minute fashion spectacle celebrating the 40th anniversary of his namesake house. The presentation featured 300 designs organized around six themes, including pop art and glamour, bringing some of Yves Saint Laurent’s most iconic creations to one of the world’s biggest stages.
From his groundbreaking women’s tuxedos to collections inspired by the bold colors of Matisse and Picasso, the show offered a sweeping look at four decades of the designer’s influence. Staged on the same day as the World Cup final, it remains one of fashion’s most ambitious runway productions—and one of the most memorable intersections of sport, fashion, and culture.
Photos Getty Images
#fifaworldcup2026 #fashionhistory

posts.caption:
The modern-day #LilithFair? That’s exactly what many fans are calling @oliviarodrigo’s new all-female music festival, Daisy Chain Fields Festival.
Ever since the lineup was announced, conversations about the iconic women-led festival of the 1990s have resurfaced. Co-founded by @officialsarahmclachlan in 1997, Lilith Fair was born out of her frustration with an industry that often sidelined female artists. The groundbreaking festival exclusively featured women performers and women-led bands, helping redefine what a major music festival could look like.
Now, nearly three decades later, McLachlan is returning to the festival stage as a special guest at Daisy Chain Fields Festival this summer, alongside artists including @stevienicks and @chappellroan.
When we asked McLachlan about Lilith Fair’s legacy, she said its impact continues to resonate with women who attended the festival years ago. “I’ve had women come up to me saying, ‘I went to Lilith in the ’90s and you showed me that I could be anything I wanted to be.’” As Daisy Chain Festival prepares to launch this summer, its connection to Lilith Fair feels undeniable — not just as a celebration of women in music, but as a continuation of a legacy that helped inspire generations of artists and fans.
Photos Getty Images
#oliviarodrigo #oliviarodrigomusic #sarahmclachlan

posts.caption:
@Balenciaga dropped its Spring Summer 2026 preview collection in Mumbai last night, and the energy in the room felt different.
With Fatima Sana Sheikh, Bobby Deol, Mouni Roy, Sanya Malhotra, Malavika Mohanan, and Krithi Shetty in attendance, it felt less like a formal showcase and more like a real moment around fashion. The room was sharp, observant, and engaged.
Fatima Sana Shaikh said it best: “Balenciaga always has a chic approach and the brand is now heading towards it even more aggressively.” And that tone carried through the evening. Nothing felt loud for attention, yet everything commanded it.
What stood out was how people responded. There was curiosity, closer inspection, and genuine interest in the details. Some silhouettes felt familiar but more refined, while others brought a quiet sense of drama without trying too hard.
That is when a collection works.
Not when it demands attention, but when it earns it.
@balenciaga #Balenciaga

posts.caption:
Jacob & Co. continues to blur the line between watchmaking, sculpture, and fantasy with the Astronomia “Gates of Olympus.”
Priced at $1.4 million, the one-of-one creation draws inspiration from Greek mythology, featuring Zeus, Olympus, celestial movement, and a fully mechanical miniature world built directly into the timepiece itself.
Inside the 18K rose gold case sits a triple-axis tourbillon, over 9 carats of baguette diamonds, and an intricate Olympus scene designed as part of the movement.
More than a watch, the piece reflects how modern haute horology is increasingly moving toward collectible art and immersive storytelling.
@JacobAndCo #JacobAndCo #Luxurious #Collab

posts.caption:
Bugatti has taken its pursuit of craftsmanship from the road to the golf course.
In collaboration with Japanese luxury golf brand Honma, the marque has unveiled an ultra-exclusive collection of golf clubs inspired by the Bugatti Tourbillon. Handcrafted by master artisans, the collection blends precision engineering with luxury design, with the rarest 5-Star edition limited to just 20 sets worldwide.
For Bugatti, it’s another reminder that luxury isn’t confined to automobiles. It’s a philosophy that can extend to every passion.
Image: @honmagolf @bugatti

posts.caption:
Burberry is bringing its signature British style to the Greek Riviera this summer through a collaboration with One&Only Aesthesis.
From June 16 to October 31, the beachfront resort will be transformed with immersive Burberry experiences, blending Mediterranean luxury with the house’s iconic design language. Set against the backdrop of the Aegean Sea, the partnership reflects the growing trend of luxury fashion brands creating destination experiences beyond the boutique.
It’s another example of fashion and hospitality coming together to redefine luxury travel.
@Burberry #Burberry #Luxurious

posts.caption:
Getting a table at Papa’s has become one of Mumbai’s most exclusive dining challenges.
With just 12 seats and reservations disappearing in under a minute each month, the Bandra restaurant has become one of India’s most sought-after culinary experiences. Founded by Hunger Inc. Hospitality as a tribute to the late chef Floyd Cardoz, Papa’s combines an intimate chef’s counter with a tasting menu that has earned global attention.
For many diners, securing a reservation has become almost as rewarding as the meal itself.
@papasbombay #Luxurious

posts.caption:
#ManifestCover
There is a quiet power to Rukmini Vasanth (@rukmini_vasanth). One that doesn’t seek attention, yet holds it effortlessly.
For Manifest’s May–June cover, she steps into an imaginative world where temple jewellery meets contemporary silhouettes, blurring the lines between memory and modernity. She embodies a woman in transition, grounded, luminous, and yet constantly becoming.
“I’m not very good at describing myself,” she says, almost in passing.
Yet, in the same breath, her choices reveal a quiet clarity of intent. “I don’t want to isolate my character. I need to understand the whole world it exists in.”
Between restraint and reinvention, stillness and movement, she exists in that rare in-between. Not fully defined, not confined. Just on the cusp of a wider sky.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story from the link in bio.
Chairperson: Avarna Jain (@avarna_jain)
CEO: Debashish Ghosh
Editor: Aindrila Mitra (@aindrilamitra)
Interview: Akshay Kaushal (@akshay_kaushal)

Fashion assistant: Shubhi Kumar (@shubhi.kumar)
Hair: Rohit Bhatkar (@rohit_bhatkar)
Makeup: Riviera Lynn (@rivieralynn)

posts.alt_text:
When your skin is “acting up,” it’s probably just asking for the right treatment.

Most common skin concerns, whether it’s acne, pigmentation, dehydration can’t be fixed with a one-product routine. They require targeted treatments that work beneath the surface, stimulating collagen, calming inflammation, boosting hydration or regenerating skin cells, depending on what your skin actually needs.

That’s exactly why we’ve got experts to suggest some of the best scientifically curated backed skincare treatments they actually recommend in their clinics.

To know more about what each treatment does, who it’s for, how many sessions you might need, head to the link in our bio.🔗

By Anushree Sardesai

[Oily skin, Dry Skin, Skin Care and Routine, Skin Specialist, SPF]

posts.caption:
Animal prints are the hottest trend of the season. Leopard, zebra, tiger, and even giraffe prints are making their way into Indian occasion wear—and we’re not mad about it. From statement sarees to bold lehengas and playful blouses, skin prints are proving they can be just as festive as florals or traditional brocades.
And the best part? You don’t have to go all in. Designers and celebrities are already embracing the trend in their own way and it’s time we hopped onto it too.
[Fashion Trends 2026, Bridal and Occasion Wear, SS26]

posts.caption:
Delhi just served up an ace to Wimbledon (@wimbledon), and it’s as delicious as it gets! The tennis tournament’s much loved strawberries and cream is now made special for Delhi by Kuremal’s Kulfi (@kuremalskulfi).
The All England Lawn Tennis Club teamed up with Delhi’s most beloved Kuremal’s Kulfi to turn its most iconic snack into a Strawberries & Cream Kulfi BUT on a stick. A British summer tradition meets one of India’s most loved desserts, and somehow, it makes perfect sense.
That said, Kuremal’s is a legacy brand that has been a part of the Indian wedding menu for many decades, long before this global spotlight found them. From watermelon kulfis to stuffed mango bites, guava sorbets to apple creations, the brand has spent years turning the chilled treat into a conversation starter at celebrations.
Read more via the link in bio🔗
By Srishti Mukherjee (@sri.sshhhhh)

posts.caption:
Telling tales from the ancient past of 1990s, the comedian Guy Branum is teaching Gen Z what their forefathers knew.
@guybranum’s video series “What the Old Gays Remember,” is a highlight reel from before 2000, when gay marriage was barely talked about, when few celebrities were out, and when drag queens had not yet sashayed their way into prime time.
With his videos, Branum is showcasing a different moment, and what it meant to claim those slivers of culture as his own. He tells campy stories like the time Celine Dion upstaged Aretha Franklin (“like an idiot,” he said) at VH1’s “Divas Live” in 1998. But he also reflects in earnest on the bodybuilding star Bob Paris, who gave up his career in 1989 by coming out of the closet.
He has embraced the role of teacher but “this is not journalism,” he told The New York Times. “This is history told to you through the telephone.” At the link in our bio, read more about how Branum is imparting his knowledge to young gays. Photos by @michellegroskopf
posts.alt_text:
After the premiere of “The Invite,” a snappy comedy about a tense marriage that stars Olivia Wilde, Rogen, Cruz and Edward Norton, a buzzy crowd gathered at the Chateau Marmont in West Hollywood to celebrate.

The party was a victory tour for Wilde’s third major directorial effort, which she said was partly influenced by her high-profile relationships and breakups. Speaking on the red carpet, Wilde said she wanted to explore the idea of “taking responsibility for your own happiness and, within relationships, never getting to a place where you’ve lost your identity.”

At the party, Cruz and her husband, Javier Bardem, mingled with fellow guests like Dakota Fanning, Kesha and Rashida Jones, who wrote “The Invite” with Will McCormack. On the patio, guests enjoyed signature cocktails and charcuterie while Seth Rogen and his wife shared a poolside joint. At the link in our bio, get an inside look at the after-party and read more about the film's premiere. Photos by @alishajucevic

He tells campy stories like the time Celine Dion upstaged Aretha Franklin (“like an idiot,” he said) at VH1’s “Divas Live” in 1998. But he also reflects in earnest on the bodybuilding star Bob Paris, who gave up his career in 1989 by coming out of the closet.

He has embraced the role of teacher but “this is not journalism,” he told The New York Times. “This is history told to you through the telephone.” At the link in our bio, read more about how Branum is imparting his knowledge to young gays. Photos by @michellegroskopf

posts.caption:
For the last several days, our fashion reporter @jacobwgallagher has been in Milan for this season’s men’s fashion shows. Here's a recap of his reviews of the shows to know and of the trends to look out for.
From would-be cabana boys to a Rugby revival, see more of the highlights from the shows at the link in our bio. And subscribe to The Fashions newsletter to get dispatches from more runways straight to your inbox. Photos by Prada, Ralph Lauren, Thom Browne, Nicola Marfisi via @apnews, Alessandro Garofalo/Reuters

posts.caption:
Remember how things looked in 2016? Brands like Warby Parker and Everlane introduced themselves in tidy, sans-serif typefaces. Pastels, especially millennial pink, surged onto Instagram feeds and Glossier mascara tubes.
Lately, something misshapen and unruly has been rising in its place. Let’s call it hyper goo: a glitchy, gloppy, maximalist mash-up of surreal smoothness and digital degradation. Companies trying to reach younger audiences are turning to colorful, inflated-looking typography that recalls the 1970s and pixelated nods to the 1990s internet.
The effect is both nostalgic and playful, offering a tempting alternative to the disillusionment that comes with being a young person today. But there may be another reason this irreverent style has struck a chord: the rise of A.I.
Shreya Murthy, the chief executive of Partiful, the event-invitation service beloved by Gen Z, said that “for us and for a lot of other companies, we think that loud and messy and customizable and, honestly, an unserious aesthetic, that’s how you prove a human actually made this.” Read more about how hyper goo is transforming consumer culture at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
As a designer of women’s clothing for 15 years, Simone Rocha has been committed to presenting a version of womanhood that subverts traditional feminine tropes, a practice she calls “pretty and perverse.” But as the culture reaches its peak “masculinity crisis” moment, @simonerocha_ is now turning more of her attention to men’s wear — focusing more on men’s softer side.
“When I started thinking about the men’s very seriously, I wanted it to feel grounded but also quite tender,” she told the New York Times reporter @yolamzizi.
Rocha has been designing men’s clothing since 2022, though until now, they have mostly served as counterpart to the women’s collections. Now she's giving men's wear its own space. This week, Rocha presented her first solo men’s show at Pitti Immagine Uomo, the biannual Italian trade show for men’s clothing. Will men bite?
Tap the link in our bio to read our full profile of Rocha and learn more about the collection came together. Photo by @jeremiesouteyrat

#JonathanAnderson’s latest #Dior Mens collection was a masterclass in craftsmanship. What may look like ordinary printed textiles were objects of exceptional couture craftsmanship.
Look 20, a black and white spotted coat, from a distance, appeared to be a simple polka-dot (stingray?) fabric, yet on closer inspection revealed a painstakingly hand embroidered textile created from thousands of individually applied beads.

The trompe-l’œil bow shirts embroidered by @lesage were inspired by a 1979 #HauteCouture dress by #MarcBohan, reimagining heritage for a modern menswear audience.

posts.alt_text:
What better way to cool down than a trip down memory lane to the #Chanel Spring 2018 waterfalls?

#KarlLagerfeld drew inspiration from the Gorges du Verdon in southern France and enlisted @atelierdevineau to create a cliff stretching approximately 85 metres across and rising around 15 metres high inside the Grand Palais. The set was complete with caves from which models emerged, while multiple functioning waterfalls poured down.

posts.caption:
There’s nothing better than a cohesive dialogue between mens and womenswear, and #SarahBurton’s debut #Givenchy menswear collection translates the visual language she’s established in her women’s collections into a wardrobe for the Givenchy gentleman.
Look closely and you’ll spot familiar details, tailored suiting is softened by lush floral embroideries from the viral spring 2026 blooms, gemstone cufflinks nod to the chandelier top, and playful metal figurine pendants recreate miniature runway looks.
For the first #menswear presentation since 2024, Sarah Burton ushers in a new era creating a house identity that feels thoughtful, modern, and unmistakably cohesive.

posts.caption:
After almost five years in Paris, #AlexanderMcQueen has announced that it will be returning home, bringing its Spring/Summer 2027 show back to #LondonFashionWeek this September.
A true homecoming, some of Lee’s most extraordinary runway finales were created in #London, the city where his vision first captivated the fashion world. An emotional climax of the story, His shows often ended with moments that blurred the boundaries between fashion, theatre, performance art and technology, leaving audiences stunned and creating images that are still discussed decades later.

posts.caption:
One of my favourite, and most hauntingly beautiful details from #DanielRoseberry’s #Schiaparelli couture collections was the sheer crinoline veils/face masks, from Fall 2024. Wrapped around the face and head they functioned as sculptural forms. The material appeared almost weightless, creating distortions that blurred the contours of the face and transformed the models into embryonic works of art. Makeup by @patstingz, hair by @guidopalau
Also reminded me of #GarethPugh’s Spring 2013 collection, which featured sheer mesh and stretched fabrics wrapped around the head and face, or #MarcJacobs Spring 2019. Major inspo for brides looking for an unconventional wedding veil!

posts.caption:
For the grand finale of her wedding to #CallumTurner, #DuaLipa looked to none other than #Chanel for her wedding dress. The custom #HauteCouture creation marked a historic moment for the house as the first-ever bridal look by #MatthieuBlazy.
The dress which was made in Paris was entirely hand-embroidered with 480,000 beads by @ateliermontex. It featured a two-meter train which was underlined with 25,000 feathers by @maison_lemarie
Images courtesy of @davidsimsofficial

posts.caption:
Justin Bieber for Issue 01: “The First Ever” Outlander Magazine In-Print!
This is a real piece of history for us all. The past 10 years of lessons and learning have led up to this moment and I couldn’t be happier to share it with you all.
Some of you may be new followers who discovered us last week and some of you may have been here since day 1, either way this is for everyone who aligns with what it means to be an outlander.
Also, this is a great time to introduce myself for the first time in 10 years. My name is @callum.mccaff & we are all Outlander. I was born in 1996 so we priced the magazine at £19.96.
Available worldwide at www.outlandermag.com
PRINT ISN’T DEAD.
🫂 @lilbieber
📸 @sweetcian
🔗 @freenjoy
posts.alt_text:
Naomi Osaka for Outlander Magazine wearing her custom Nike Roland Garros looks designed by Nike & Kevin Germanier!

Roland Garros’ best dressed just raised the bar again. Naomi’s custom Nike looks were designed by Kevin Germanier, the couture designer turning upcycled materials into something iconic. Clay court season needed a moment like this, so we took her to an authentic orange clay court in paris to match the energy she’ll be surrounded by this week😤🤝

posts.caption:
Travis Scott for Issue 01: “The First Ever” Outlander Magazine In-Print!
GANG, TRAVIS SCOTT IN HERMÈS FOR OUR FIRST EVER MAGAZINE COVER. THIS IS UNHEARD OF, AND BETTER YET? IT WAS FOR OUTLANDER MAGAZINE. I AM HUMBLED.
WOW, what a moment. My only wish in this life is that what I’ve spent the last 10 years building acts as a strong form of inspiration for your own creative careers - I came from no fashion background, I had no connections & I had no idea what I was doing, but, I did it anyway.
If you didn’t know by now, my name is @callum.mccaff , I founded Outlander - and I’m here to remind everyone that PRINT ISN’T DEAD.
Available worldwide at www.outlandermag.com

Some of you may be new followers who discovered us last week and some of you may have been here since day 1, either way this is for everyone who aligns with what it means to be an outlander. 

Also, this is a great time to introduce myself for the first time in 10 years. My name is @callum.mccaff & we are all Outlander. I was born in 1996 so we priced the magazine at £19.96.

Available worldwide at www.outlandermag.com 

PRINT ISN’T DEAD.

🫂 @lilbieber 
📸 @sweetcian 
🔗 @freenjoy

posts.caption:
India's Renee Noronha has won first place in the 18-24 category at the Ironman Philippines. Completing the gruelling challenge at 15:55:37, the 20-year-old IIT Madras student upheld held her record of being the youngest Indian female Ironman finisher.
Renee battled torrential rains and unforgiving temperatures to complete the race. In conversation with SheThePeople, the triathlete also revealed that she participated on day 3 of her period, making the victory a profound reminder of her strength and resilience as a woman.
With this win, Renee has now qualified for the Ironman World Championship Hawaii, scheduled for October 2026. She is also on a mission to set a new Guinness world record to become the youngest person in the world to do a full Ironman on 6 continents. She is on track to achieve this by April 2027, at the age of 21.
Read full interview at www.shethepeople.tv. | @renee.noronha
#Ironman #Philippines #sports full Ironman challenge, women in sports, women in endurance sports, mumbai sports, Chennai sports, IIT Madras student, women in STEM, ironman Kona, triathlon, marathon, menstrual awareness

posts.caption:
India's women's 4x100m relay team produced a tactical masterclass to clinch a sensational Gold on the final day of the Asian Relay Championships 2026 on Sunday.
The quartet of SS Sneha, Sudeshna Shivankar, Tamanna, and veteran anchor Srabani Nanda executed a technically flawless race, clocking a season-best 43.85 seconds.
Sharp baton exchanges and explosive finishing speed propelled them past hosts China (44.09 seconds) and Thailand (44.11 seconds), who settled for silver and bronze respectively in a thrilling three-way battle.

posts.caption:
During the phase of the Mars Orbiter Mission, Indian space scientist Nandini Harinath wore a red and blue silk saree, which is now being talked about thousands of kilometres away from India.  The saree is on display at the Smithsonian National Air and Space Museum in Washington, DC.
The museum revealed how a saree was displayed because “rocket woman” Nandini Harinath wore it to work the day the Indian Space Research Organisation’s (ISRO) spacecraft successfully left Earth’s orbit to begin its 300-day journey to Mars.
Nandini Harinath was born to a maths teacher mother and an engineer father. She has supported more than 14 missions in her career at the Indian space agency.
#isro #science Indian Space and Research Organisation, Nandini Harinath, women in STEM, Mangalyaan, US museum, Indian scientist, India, viral, trending, traditional attire, Rocket woman

posts.caption:
Just imagine, a girl posting a selfie while crying with perfect lighting and a caption like ‘healing but still hurting’. Is this normal? Does real pain look like this? Or is it just for attention? Social media has 'aestheticised' pain, glorifying and showcasing it in a very visually appealing manner rather than in its raw form. It shifts the focus from how the pain feels to how it looks.
The rise of content creation has made everything a part of content to the extent that pain is scripted, edited and then posted as relatable videos. The emotions created attract engagement and become a part of the aesthetic.
To make the aesthetics, the online platforms use dim lights, warm tone, and backgrounds like empty roads, night sky, and take shots which are cinematic and slow motioned, pairing with lo-fi tracks and poetic script.
Visit www.shethepeople.tv to read more.
#sadness #aesthetic #lanadelrey aesthetics, pain, content, social media, online platforms, Lana del rey, billie eilish, Kabir Singh, bollywood films, performative sadness, expressions, sensitivity

posts.caption:
When life was falling apart, love found a way.
Manish and Nitesh met at a time when both were seeking comfort, companionship, and clarity - and what began as an unexpected connection quietly turned into the promise of a lifetime. From a sweet text proposal to a pastel-hued wedding on a serene farm, their journey was filled with warmth, honesty, and unwavering support.
A celebration not just of love, but of courage, acceptance, and new beginnings.
And then there was them. ❤️
But what made this wedding truly unforgettable was the strength behind its softness - two people choosing each other openly, surrounded by friends and family who showed up with nothing but joy, beneath drapes of white and blooms that mirrored the gentleness of their story. Nothing rushed. Nothing hidden. Just love, exactly as it deserved to be celebrated.
Some weddings mark a beginning.
And some mark an arrival - the courage it took to get here, the people who stood beside them through it, and a love story that proves the promise of a lifetime can begin at any point in it. 🌟
Couple: @escapewithrawnish
Venue: @casalontano_stays
Decor: @theweddingeve
Catering: @barbequenation

posts.caption:
A Bumble match worth 99 miles. A helicopter proposal in Mallorca. And a wedding that looked like it walked straight out of a Bollywood blockbuster.
Kinjal and Herit’s love story was never going to be ordinary - and their wedding at Villa Balbiano made sure of it. Couture lehengas by Tarun Tahiliani, Michelin-star dining, and a first dance taken in the rain with lightning striking between the Italian mountains. The kind of moment a director would have to fight for.
Theirs just happened.
And then there was them. ❤️
But what made this celebration truly extraordinary was the intimacy at its core - a Hindu ceremony held with just nine guests, proving that a wedding doesn’t need scale to be magical, only the right people in the right place. Lake Como provided the grandeur. Kinjal and Herit provided the rest.
Some love stories are written.
And some simply happen - a swipe, a helicopter, a storm over the Italian Alps, and a couple who turned every bit of it into the most iconic celebration imaginable. 🌟
Couple: Kinjal & Herit

posts.caption:
Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.
It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.
And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.
Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.
Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.
That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.
➡️➡️ If you are a working fashion stylist who is ready to do exactly that, comment STUDIO and we will send you the free trial link to The Studio of Stylist Elixir. Rate guides. Contracts. Portfolio tools. Masterclasses every Monday. Everything you need to build a career that reflects what you are actually worth.
Save this. Send it to a stylist who needs to hear it.
posts.alt_text:
Jonathan Anderson just showed his first couture collection for Dior. If you do not understand why it mattered, this post is for you.

John Galliano was front row. The artisans were the opening act. The silhouettes came from ceramicist Magdalene Odundo.

If you are in Paris: the show space at Musée Rodin opens to the public from 28 January for one week. The new couture collection, archival Dior pieces, and the Odundo ceramics that inspired it. You can see it yourself.

This is not about liking or not liking a collection. This is about understanding what you are looking at.
The Studio of Stylist Elixir is our online membership. Guides. Templates. Live calls. A community of stylists who are learning and building together. Comment ‘studio’ for a free trial.

Save this. Share it with someone who needs it.

#dior #jonathananderson #stylistelixir

posts.caption:
After enough years you can spot each of these within five minutes.
The one who says « effortless » and means three hours of work. The one with forty Pinterest screenshots on forty different bodies. And every so often, the one who starts as a job and ends up family.
Send this to the stylist who would get it. They have met every one too.
Now the real talk. Knowing your clients is easy. Booking better ones is a different skill, and it is the one no one ever taught us.
That is what The Studio is. It is my membership for working stylists, the room I wish I had when I started. Inside you get the rate breakdown from over a thousand stylists so you stop guessing your worth, the pitch templates that actually get replies, masterclasses on portfolio, sourcing and client work, a new live event every month, and a hundred plus stylists on hand when you are stuck. It is everything between having the eye and actually being booked for it.
Right now it is £20 a month, cancel anytime. In August it becomes The BOOKED Method and the price more than doubles, and everyone already inside keeps the £20. Comment STUDIO or tap the link in my bio.
Day 18 of 30 🤍
#fashionstylist #personalstylist #fashionstyling #stylistlife #clientstories

The most expensive habit in styling isn’t bad work. It’s dropping your rate the second a client goes quiet.
Say the number. Then hold it.
If you want to stop guessing what to charge and build a sustainable styling career, comment BUILD and I’ll show you how we can help.

posts.caption:
There is no one path to getting booked. Everyone’s road looks different, and yours running differently to someone else’s does not mean you are behind.
The stylists who get there are not luckier or more connected. They follow a method. They get focused, build the proof, make themselves easy to find, reach out, follow up, and stay in the conversation, steadily, until it becomes how they work.
That whole path, Foundations, Portfolio, Craft and Kit, Visibility and Pitching, Rates, Boundaries and Protection, Creative Direction, is The BOOKED Method, landing this August. It lives inside The Studio now at £20 a month, and when it launches the price more than doubles.
Comment STUDIO and I’ll send you everything.
Day 16 of 30 🤍

posts.caption:
Fashion styling has a rate, and the industry keeps it deliberately vague. That silence protects clients, not stylists.
So we polled over 1,200 working stylists across the UK, US and Europe and asked what they actually charge. Day rates, usage, prep, per look, broken down by experience and by sector. The real numbers, not guesswork.
We have put it all into a free PDF guide. Comment RATES and we will DM it straight over, and add you to our newsletter for more. Unsubscribe anytime.
Stylist Elixir, Emily
#fashionstylist #stylistrates #fashionstyling #personalstylist

posts.caption:
India talks about caring for ageing parents as culture. But the systems around that care still carry shame, suspicion and serious gaps.
An old age home in India invites judgement. A senior citizen caretaker raises questions of trust. Apps promise independence, even as many older Indians remain vulnerable to scams, isolation and anxiety.
As families become smaller and more dispersed, elderly care in India is entering unfamiliar territory. Senior citizen care in India still revolves around duty, while the reality of ageing demands more: better healthcare for senior citizens, stronger support systems, technology designed for people who did not grow up online and, most of all, dignity.
Ideally, the gap can be filled with tech. But the only tech that can help the elderly gain some independence in India is one that detects when something is amiss and sends help automatically.
Read more at the link in bio.
By Karishma Kuenzang (@kkuenzang)
#AgeingIndia #ElderlyCare #AgeTech #IndianFamilies #SeniorLiving
posts.alt_text:
In the latest edition of The Established’s In The Studio series, we step inside the world of JJ Valaya (@jjvalaya), who launched his label in 1992, at a time when Indian fashion was still building its own design language. Three decades later, his world remains instantly recognisable: royal, nomadic, cinematic and deeply rooted in craft.
 
From couture and bridalwear to JJV Kapurthala, Muse, photography and Valaya Home, the House of Valaya is a fully realised maison-style universe. His work draws from history without feeling trapped by it, turning old-world references into something disciplined, modern and collectible.
 
Inside the studio, that process comes alive. Ideas move from swatches, sketches and samples to the atelier floor and Valaya’s world remains instantly recognisable: royal, nomadic, cinematic and deeply rooted in craft.

posts.caption:
Everyone has taste now, allegedly.
A slick bun. A smoke cocktail. A microcement wall. A padel outfit worn nowhere near a court.
Instagram aesthetic has made everyone do the same things differently enough to call it personal taste.
This is tasteslop: aesthetic signals stripped of context, repeated until they feel hollow. The term was coined by cultural theorist Emily Segal for a world where good taste has become easier to copy than understand.
Artificial intelligence has only made that repetition faster.
So what happens when everyone has learnt the same language of good taste?
Read more at the link in bio.
By Karishma Kuenzang (@kkuenzang)
#InstagramAesthetic #Tasteslop #AlgorithmicTaste #CulturalCapital #Originality

posts.caption:
Motherhood is moving later because women have had to make room for everything else first: work, money, independence, partnership, choice.
IVF, egg freezing and better fertility care have made delayed parenthood more possible. Biology, however, has not adjusted its timeline.
For many women having children in their late 30s and early 40s, postpartum recovery can sit uncomfortably close to perimenopause. Fatigue, anxiety, sleep disruption and brain fog can start to look like motherhood, ageing, or both.
That is the blind spot. Women are expected to absorb hormonal change without always having the language, diagnosis or care to name what is happening.
The conversation around women’s health has to catch up with the reality of women’s lives.
Read more at the link in bio.
By Ria Bhatia (@riabbhatia)

posts.caption:
Summer has always shaped life in India. What is changing is the extent to which life now has to be organised around heat.
People are leaving work earlier, staying out later, sleeping on floors, changing their diets, adjusting their school attendance, and spending more of their income on cooling.
These decisions are often framed as adaptation, yet many emerge from necessity rather than choice. For those who work outdoors, miss a day’s wage if they stay home, or cannot afford air conditioning, there is little room to negotiate with rising temperatures.
Heat leaves few dramatic images behind. It does not wash away roads or flatten buildings. Its effects accumulate in the body, in lost income, disrupted sleep, worsening health and shrinking margins for recovery.
As hotter days are followed by hotter nights, how long can individual households keep absorbing a crisis they did not create?
Read more at the link in bio.
By Arshia Dhar (@urseaa)
#HeatwaveIndia #ClimateCrisis #ExtremeHeat #ClimateChange #SummerInIndia

posts.caption:
@dualipa is opening a library. That’s the headline.
Launching June 27 in Porto, the Manifesto Library will live inside Livraria Lello, featuring 100 titles that challenge power, censorship, and dominant narratives—from The Handmaid’s Tale to Reginald Dwayne Betts’ Felon.
It’s a natural extension of @service95, Lipa’s editorial platform, where she’s built a reputation not just as a reader, but as a serious literary interlocutor—interviewing writers like Olga Tokarczuk and showing up in spaces most celebrities wouldn’t think to enter, from prize stages to prison book clubs.
Call it unexpected, or call it intentional. While most celebrity “side projects” orbit vanity, Lipa certainly knows how to use her platform for the best of the best.
#DuaLipa #Service95
posts.alt_text:
The fashion industry has always traded in the currency of spectacle, but our current era has birthed a new sub-genre: mindless provocation masquerading as marketing genius. Yesterday, internet personality and “looksmaxxer” Clavicular (Braden Peters) opened the 424 Men’s SS27 runway show at Paris Fashion Week, which marks his second major runway appearance after his controversial debut closing Elena Velez FW26 in New York.

The fashion industry’s ongoing flirtation with Clavicular in particular is a clear marker of a culture that has traded creative vision for algorithmic relevance. What do these stunts and others like them say about the state of the state of the broader industry?

Read the latest on Substack: The Citizen’s Poste | posteposteposte.com

Images: @424inc, Guillermo Andrade

#Substack #ParisFashionWeek

posts.caption:
Jonathan Anderson’s second SS menswear collection for Dior arrived in Paris amid a sweltering heat wave.
Part English eccentricity, part French couture, the collection felt like a cast of aristocratic partygoers stumbling home at sunrise. Anderson described the collection as a fantasy character: someone concerned with dressing up, going out, and embracing a certain theatricality. The references were layered: echoes of rock-star glamour, British boarding-school codes, and Dior’s own menswear history all filtered through Anderson’s distinctly intellectual lens.
A year into his Dior tenure, the biggest takeaway is that Anderson is no longer introducing himself.
And what do we think?
#Dior #JonathanAnderson #ParisFashionWeek #Menswear #DiorMen

posts.caption:
If there was any doubt that @thombrowne remains one of menswear’s great storytellers, his Milan return put it to rest. Staged in the courtyard of Palazzo Serbelloni beneath a sweltering Italian sun, Browne’s first standalone menswear show since 2023 was stunning.
Drawing inspiration from A Bug’s Life and the fairytale of the prince and the frog, Browne filled the runway with dragonflies, bees, spiders, crickets, and gilded beehives rendered through extraordinary embroidery and embellishment. Beneath the whimsy, however, was a collection firmly rooted in what he does best: impeccable tailoring, rigorous construction, and a deeply personal interpretation of American prep.
Images: Vogue; Backgrid
#ThomBrowne #MilanFashionWeek #Menswear #FashionWeek #TheCitizensPoste

posts.alt_text:
for spring/summer 2027 pre-collection, Stefano Gallici isn’t chasing a muse or recreating a decade. instead, he’s collecting moments: jackets that feel discovered rather than designed, dresses that drift somewhere between Edwardian romance and modern ease, faded denim, worn leathers, floral devoré textures and tailoring that feels a little sharper, a little leaner.

pink quietly interrupts black. brown leather meets pinstripes. light-washed denim picks up yellow stains like souvenirs. it’s romantic without trying too hard, nostalgic without looking backwards.

the best Ann Demeulemeester collections (such as this one) give you a life you suddenly want to disappear into.

#TFO #AnnDemeulemeester #StefanoGallici #SS27

presented inside the house at 3 Avenue George V, Sarah Burton’s latest menswear collection unfolded across three rooms, each dedicated to a different way of dressing.
rather than chasing trends, Burton is building a wardrobe with permanence. clothes that feel lived in, personal and full of meaning. set against Rachel Whiteread’s quietly powerful works, the presentation felt less like a fashion show and more like stepping into someone’s world.

posts.caption:
Fatherhood is a new role for the actor who first got noticed for his performance in ‘Masaan’ (2015). In fact, it seems this off-camera Vicky Kaushal (@vickykaushal09) is perhaps closer to Deepak Chaudhary, the character he played in the film. A sensitive, ambitious young man coming to terms with the world around him. That character, Kaushal says, changed him. But not for the obvious reasons.
“Until then success, to me, was where you reached [in life]. It was never about where you started from,” he explains. “That character, starting from the crematorium ghat in Benaras and going on to work as an engineer in the Railways in Allahabad [now Prayagraj]... He had a far bigger life arc than I’ve covered in my life. Till today,” he says, tapping the arm rest for emphasis. “After that I started having so much empathy for those around me, especially those from far less privileged backgrounds, because I don’t know where they have started from.”
@travellerprasad in conversation with the Bollywood actor at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Thirty minutes after this interview was supposed to begin, Vicky Kaushal’s (@vickykaushal09) tall, statuesque frame bursts in through the door at the Matrix office in Khar, Mumbai. “So sorry, bro,” he says, shaking this reporter’s hand with both of his. His day has been busy—his son’s visa was to be sorted, he couldn’t leave his wife alone, and there’s repair work going on in his building, he says, sounding like your average young husband and father with his daily household chores to take care of, not some Bollywood star with a platoon of staff to handle his every whim.
Since becoming a father, Kaushal says he’s come to understand and appreciate “a lot more” of the work his parents did in raising him. “It occurs to me at the most random moments that that [instilling the right values] is now my responsibility towards my son,” he says. “He is going to learn by observing how his parents are thinking, talking, and behaving. So, I’ve realised whatever you want to see reflected in him has to come in your actions rather than words.”
@travellerprasad in conversation with the Bollywood actor at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Hailey Bieber gave us glazed-doughnut skin. Ava Lee gave us Jell-O skin. The jelly mist is the spray that promises to get you there by lunch. And admit it: your feed is drowning in it. The actual product is a hydrating serum with a jelly texture that sprays as a fine veil and doesn’t vanish the second it lands. It sets into a thin gel film that holds moisture instead of lifting off in the next blast of AC.
But, at a moment when we are considering deleting our UPI apps just to curb the spending, do you really need a jelly mist if you already own a mist? Your skin in July isn’t short on water. It does, however, need relief. A jelly mist cools you and resets your face at 4 pm without sliding your makeup into your collar.
If you’re looking for something calming that survives our humid afternoons, give jelly mists a try. @ritusom’s edit at the link in bio.
@dr.melaxin_global @beekman1802

posts.caption:
Even if you haven’t watched Succession, chances are you’re familiar with the viral scene featuring the ‘ludicrously capacious bag’. Cousin Greg’s date Bridget arrives at Logan Roy’s birthday party carrying a checked Burberry tote so large, so conspicuous, and so out of step with the room’s devotion to ‘quiet luxury’, it instantly became a punchline.
Three years down the line, though, thanks to Milan and Paris Fashion Weeks, we’re witnessing quite the turnaround in our attitude to gargantuan bags. Designers showcasing their spring/summer 2027 menswear collections across the fashion capitals seem to have decided that bigger is, unequivocally, better.
At the link in bio, @sirf.anushka on the big bag’s big comeback.

posts.caption:
Hi, I’m Veronica 👋🏼 a sewing pattern designer based in Melbourne, Australia.
I create patterns, tools and resources that help you sew with understanding.
My work focuses on how fabrics behave, how garments are constructed, and how sewing connects us to the history of what we wear.
Sewing Patterns
My patterns are designed for versatility, wearability and fun. They include the Hazel, Freya, Odette, Audrey, Hera, Cordelia and the Sleeve Studio — each combining comfort with thoughtful design details. You’ll also find The Noelle, a free beginner-friendly pattern that introduces my approach to fit and construction.
The Paper Doll
A print-at-home paper doll designed to help you visualise and play with my sewing patterns. You can cut it out and colour it yourself, or use the pre-filled versions featuring my fabric prints and colour palettes. The set includes curvier model options and three skin tones to choose from.
The Conscious Consumer eBook
A practical guide to fabrics, sustainability and sewing with intention. It helps you understand fibre origins, weave structures and how to make long-lasting choices when you sew or buy clothes.
Digital Sewing Tools
Two paid calculators designed for precision:
• Shirring Calculator – works out fabric shrinkage and elastic thread length.
• GSM Calculator – estimates fabric weight and drape.
Available individually or as a discounted bundle.
My PDF sewing patterns are stocked digitally through @fabricgodmother , @spotlightstores , @thefoldline , @joans.finefabric and @thedrapersdaughter .
Fabric and Accessories
You can shop my fabric designs on @spoonflower and find my illustrated phone cases at @thedairy .
Everything I create is designed to help you sew with confidence, understanding and creativity.
For all enquires email us at hello@veronicatuckerthelabel.com
posts.alt_text:
In 1926 Vogue published a sketch of a Chanel dress and called it a Ford and that’s where the myth that Chanel invented the little black dress was born.

But four years earlier, in 1922, the house of Premet produced La Garçonne which was a simple black dress with a white collar and cuffs that Vogue called “the most celebrated single dress the world ever knew.” Fashion historian Valerie Steele documented a million copies sold globally.

The designer was almost certainly Charlotte Revyl. Head designer at Premet from 1918 to 1930. Running a house founded by a woman and led throughout its entire history by women.

At the same time, British designer Edward Molyneux was making his own simple black dresses.

Chanel got the credit because Chanel got the magazine call-out. And where Chanel survived, Premet filed bankruptcy in 1932 and Charlotte’s name disappeared with it.

This is kinda how women’s fashion history works. The innovation happens, then only one name gets attached to it and all the other names disappear.

Charlotte Revyl and Edward Molyneux deserve their flowers on this one too.

posts.caption: Hi! I'm really happy you spoke about the Orisha! I'm a Yoruba girl myself so this was really cool to see. I wanted to clarify though that orisha like Yemoja and Oshun are not goddesses. Yoruba spirituality is monotheistic. So there is only one supreme being (Eledumare). The orisha are lower (yet still very powerful) deities/spiritual beings. They are sent by Eledumare to help humans deal with various earthly matters. Some orisha have actually been human before becoming an orisha after death. An example is Oduduwa. Omg im so sorry for the long comment but this was really cool to see! Just had to make that clarification.

posts.caption:
The Odyssey is named after a man, but read it again and you’ll find the women are the ones who actually run the show.
I’ve joined @readingsbooks Big Book Club working through Homer’s Odyssey. Five sessions with academics and authors, timed to Christopher Nolan’s adaptation landing in July. This is what I learned from Session 1 with Dr Seth Robinson.
Athena engineers the entire return. Circe gives Odysseus the knowledge to navigate the underworld. Nausicaa saves him on the beach. Ino saves him from drowning. Even Calypso hands him the tools to leave. The women are the ones who hold all the knowledge and the so-called monsters he defeats with it, Scylla, Charybdis, the Sirens, were only ever defending their own space.
And then there’s Penelope. Weaving by day, unpicking it by night, buying herself time with the one form of power she’s allowed…the loom and the sacred space it wields for her.
But the part that has stayed with me most was the translation. Robinson showed two versions of the same passage from Book 22, the killing of the maids. One translator calls them women, but Emily Wilson calls them girls. One frames it as deserved; the other shows you the pain. Translation is never an unbiased, neutral act. It will always tell you something about the person doing it, and the time they’re living in.
So here’s the question I’m sitting with: if this is the foundational text of the Western world, what does it say about us now?
Full essay on Substack 🔗 link in bio. Session 2 goes deeper into the women: Penelope, Helen, Calypso, Circe, Nausicaa.
What did you think of The Odyssey, if you’ve read it? Tell me below 👇🏼
In partnership with @readingsbooks

As the freshly minted face of @MuglerOfficial's Angel Blush Eau de Parfum, a new take on a beloved fragrance rolling out nationwide this fall, Schafer shares that working with the brand is a dream. “I was watching those hourlong runway shows that you can find on YouTube when I was in high school,” she says, adding that Mugler was always an “enormous sort of vault of inspiration” for her. At the link in bio, the actor reflects with Vogue on the end of "Euphoria," and how she's preparing for her busiest year yet. Photos: @carlijnjacobs
posts.alt_text:
Chaos is something @GracieAbrams has grown accustomed to. Eight years ago, she was a first-year student at Barnard with a cult fandom she’d fostered through Instagram; soon she was signed to Interscope, then on her first tour, then opening for Olivia Rodrigo and Taylor Swift. By the end of 2024, Abrams was a Grammy-nominated pop star filling arenas.

She finally took a breather last summer. “It’s not until you stop that you realize ‘Oh, I haven’t seen my friends in a long time. I haven’t cooked a meal in a long time,’” Abrams says. “The sleep schedule becomes regular for the first time in, like, four years.”

Now 26, she’s shifting into a new gear. Abrams’s third studio album, “Daughter from Hell,” out in July, sounds like her—earnest, introspective, folk-informed—but the production is more lush (“It feels like me in progress,” she says); she’s also in a happy relationship and making her first film, “Please,” with the filmmaker Halina Reijn.

For Vogue’s Summer 2026 issue, Brittany Spanos meets Gracie Abrams in Los Angeles and New York to talk about growing up, slowing down, falling in love, and where she’d like to go next. Tap the link in our bio to read the full profile. 

Photographed by @larissahofmann, Written by @brittanyspanos, Styled by @jordenbickham, Hair by @tamastuzes, Makeup by @emikaneko, Manicure by @1.800.nailme, Tailoring by Irina Tshartaryan for Susie’s Custom Designs, Produced by @hyperion.la, Set Design by  @patienceharding. Shot on location @lucasmuseum. Vogue, Summer 2026.

Fly @singaporeair to Cairns from India via Singapore.

posts.alt_text:
Chaos is something @GracieAbrams has grown accustomed to. Eight years ago, she was a first-year student at Barnard with a cult fandom she’d fostered through Instagram; soon she was signed to Interscope, then on her first tour, then opening for Olivia Rodrigo and Taylor Swift. By the end of 2024, Abrams was a Grammy-nominated pop star filling arenas.

She finally took a breather last summer. “It’s not until you stop that you realize ‘Oh, I haven’t seen my friends in a long time. I haven’t cooked a meal in a long time,’” Abrams says. “The sleep schedule becomes regular for the first time in, like, four years.”

Now 26, she’s shifting into a new gear. Abrams’s third studio album, “Daughter from Hell,” out in July, sounds like her—earnest, introspective, folk-informed—but the production is more lush (“It feels like me in progress,” she says); she’s also in a happy relationship and making her first film, “Please,” with the filmmaker Halina Reijn.

For Vogue’s Summer 2026 issue, Brittany Spanos meets Gracie Abrams in Los Angeles and New York to talk about growing up, slowing down, falling in love, and where she’d like to go next. Tap the link in our bio to read the full profile. 

Photographed by @larissahofmann, Written by @brittanyspanos, Styled by @jordenbickham, Hair by @tamastuzes, Makeup by @emikaneko, Manicure by @1.800.nailme, Tailoring by Irina Tshartaryan for Susie’s Custom Designs, Produced by @hyperion.la, Set Design by  @patienceharding. Shot on location @lucasmuseum. Vogue, Summer 2026.

posts.caption:
The first time @Eileen Kelly walked through the doors of the home she’s nicknamed Slippers, she could see her future with unsettling clarity: aqua walls in the entry, leopard carpet underfoot, a deep fuchsia living room peeking through an archway, and her bedroom awash in pink. She had rarely been so certain of anything. Together with interior designer Emily Eerdmans, Kelly embraced a fantasy of 1950s Hollywood glamour filtered through a 1920s lens. Head to the link in our bio to learn more about how Kelly transformed this Los Angeles fixer-upper into her whimsical, pattern-happy new home.
Photographed by @stefanruizphoto, Written by @eileen, Styled by @rebeccarams, Hair by @ramsell, Makeup by @courthart1, Tailoring by Susie Kourinian for Susie’s Custom Designs, Produced by @tinytomahawk. Vogue, Summer 2026.

posts.caption: There are weddings. There are celebrity weddings. And then there is the time Sly Stone decided to marry his actress fiancée, Kathy Silva, at Madison Square Garden before an audience of 23,000. For the musician who turned rock concerts into joyous, genre-defying spectacles, a church was never going to suffice—neither was a tuxedo. At the link in our bio, Vogue looks back at the couple’s unforgettable 1974 wedding at the arena currently on everyone’s minds.

posts.caption: After the conclusion of HBO's buzzy coming-of-age drama, "Euphoria," @HunterSchafer is ready for her Saturn return. As the freshly minted face of @MuglerOfficial's Angel Blush Eau de Parfum, a new take on a beloved fragrance rolling out nationwide this fall, Schafer shares that working with the brand is a dream. “I was watching those hourlong runway shows that you can find on YouTube when I was in high school,” she says, adding that Mugler was always an “enormous sort of vault of inspiration” for her. At the link in bio, the actor reflects with Vogue on the end of "Euphoria," and how she's preparing for her busiest year yet. Photos: @carlijnjacobs

posts.caption: Director Alice Winocour spent a year and a half embedding herself backstage at shows during Paris Fashion Week before writing her script for “Couture.” In doing so, she was able to observe the women behind the scenes who are rarely the subjects of attention themselves. That instinct—to turn the camera away from the runway and toward the workroom—is the subversive heart of the film, in theaters today. The movie follows a bit of a meta narrative: Angelina Jolie plays Maxine Walker, an American director making a fashion film who receives a breast cancer diagnosis mid-shoot. The film effectively takes its structure from the craft it canonizes, creating a portrait of an artist through the women with whom she interacts. It also happens to be the first narrative film ever shot inside Chanel’s haute couture atelier, and that access—not to mention the permission to erase every logo—grounds the film, unexpectedly, in a communal act of creation. Get all the details at the link in our bio.

posts.caption: Are you shrekking in your relationship? Are Jacob Elordi and Kendall Jenner the perfect swag match? By the way, are people still talking about swag gaps? Forget people, can the runways have them too? Observing the particularly funky men’s shows this season, we'd say that men’s fashion week might just be closing the swag gap with its flashier womenswear counterpart. Read more at the link in bio.
posts.alt_text: “How can we keep pushing?” This was the question @KikoKostadinov said he set himself and his team following last season’s rejection of storytelling superfluity—no characters, no narrative—and his effort to reduce garments into geometric form. The answer came through Agostino Bonalumi, an Italian artist whose canvases were shaped by structures hidden beneath their surfaces. “He constructed the canvas more like sculpture,” Kostadinov said. “So I was like, oh, we can maybe try and use some sort of structure like boning.” That became the basis of a collection in which visual emphasis was generated by construction rather than surface decoration. See every look and read the review from @luke_leitch at the link in bio. Photographed by @styledumonde

posts.caption:
#ParisFashionWeek Men’s, day four: From RomeoBeckham’s turn at @WillyChavarria to @PeterCopping’s first @Lanvin menswear presentation — plus @SharonStone closing the @Vetements_Official show. Here’s what you don’t want to miss from the fourth day of Paris.
Photo 1 by @styledumonde
posts.alt_text: When @Madonna’s management called @Grindr to propose a partnership for the launch of her upcoming album, Confessions II, the app’s chief marketing officer @Tristy_Pins knew that “re-architecting” the brand and platform from the inside — de-stigmatizing the fact it’s used for hook-ups by showing that it’s a cultural and community hub — had finally paid off. While many players stepped back from #Pride marketing in recent years, the appetite remains strong, and it’s lucrative for those who get it right. Read all about it through the link in our bio.

posts.caption: More than the clothes, the talk of every front row this men’s Fashion Week is the Paris heatwave. While most are complaining, our senior news editor @isuredontknow actually *likes* the sweltering temps. Tap the link in bio for his defense of hot weather—it’s sexy, it’s unifying, it’s memorable. Photographed by @mrstreetpeeper

posts.caption: "Saltburn," Whit Stillman’s "Metropolitan," "Brideshead Revisited." What you see in Jonathan Anderson’s @Dior Men will depend on your age and your media diet. Anderson addressed the multigenerational potential of the collection after the show: “There are things in here that my dad could wear, that I could wear,” he told @SarahMower_. “But fashion as a totemic look? I don’t know about that. I kind of enjoy screwing with it. Fashion has to be enjoyable. It has to be fun.” See every look at the link in bio. Backstage photographs by @styledumonde
